5 min read
Just now

--

You’ve seen these job titles everywhere.
UX Designer.
UI Designer.
Web Designer.
And you’re probably thinking — aren’t they all just… the same thing?
They’re not.
Not even close.
And confusing them can cost you real money when you’re hiring for your next project.
Let me break it down simply.
No jargon.
No fluff.
Just the real difference.
Start With This One Idea
Every website or app you’ve ever used has two sides.
How it works.
And how it looks.
Those are two completely different jobs.
And sometimes — a third person ties them both together into actual code.
That’s where UX, UI, and Web Design split apart.
UX Designer — The Problem Solver
UX stands for User Experience.
A UX designer doesn’t care about colors.
They don’t care about fonts.
They care about one thing — does this actually work for the user?
They ask questions like — Where does the user get confused? Why are people dropping off on this page? What’s the fastest path from point A to point B?
They sketch wireframes.
Rough, ugly, black-and-white boxes on paper or a screen.
They map out user journeys.
They run tests with real people.
They watch someone use the product and take notes.
A UX designer is basically a researcher and a strategist wrapped into one.
Their output is logic, not beauty.
If your website is confusing to navigate — you need a UX designer.
UI Designer — The Visual Artist
UI stands for User Interface.
A UI designer takes the UX designer’s wireframe — that ugly box layout — and makes it beautiful.
They pick the colors.
They choose the typography.
They design the buttons, icons, spacing, and every visual element you see on screen.
They care deeply about consistency.
Every button should look the same across the entire app.
Every heading should follow the same visual rule.
They build what’s called a design system — a rulebook of visual components.
Their output is a pixel-perfect design file — usually made in Figma or Adobe XD.
It looks exactly like the final product.
But it’s still not a real website yet.
It’s a high-quality visual blueprint.
If your website looks outdated or visually inconsistent — you need a UI designer.
Web Designer — The One Who Builds It
Now here’s where it gets interesting.
A web designer usually does both UX and UI — but at a smaller scale.
They’re not going as deep into research and user testing like a dedicated UX designer would.
They’re not building a full design system like a senior UI designer would.
But they understand both.
And most importantly — they actually build the website.
They work in tools like WordPress, Webflow, Shopify, or write HTML and CSS directly.
They take a design concept and turn it into something that actually lives on the internet.
You click it. It loads. It works.
That’s the web designer’s job.
For most small businesses, startups, and freelancers — a web designer is all you need.
You don’t need three separate people.
You need one solid web designer who can think about experience, design it well, and build it.
A Simple Analogy to Make It Click
Think of building a house.
The UX designer is the architect.
They figure out where the rooms go, how people will move through the house, and what makes logical sense.
The UI designer is the interior decorator.
They decide the colors of the walls, the furniture style, the lighting fixtures, and how everything looks and feels.
The web designer is the contractor.
They take both plans and actually build the thing.
Sometimes one person does all three.
Sometimes three different people handle each role.
It depends on the size and complexity of the project.
Which One Do You Actually Need?
If you’re a small business owner who just needs a clean website — hire a web designer.
If you’re building a complex app with multiple user flows — hire a UX designer first, then a UI designer.
If your existing website looks messy and inconsistent — hire a UI designer to clean it up.
If your existing website looks fine but people keep leaving without doing anything — hire a UX designer to diagnose the problem.
And if you want one person who can handle everything at a reasonable cost — platforms like Fiverr have talented web designers who understand UX and UI principles without charging agency rates.
The Overlap Is Real
Here’s something nobody tells you upfront.
In the real world, these roles blend constantly.
Most web designers know basic UX principles.
Most UI designers understand basic UX thinking.
Many UX designers can mockup a basic UI.
The clean separation only really exists at large companies like Google, Apple, or Meta — where teams are big enough to have specialists for everything.
For everyone else — you’re working with people who wear multiple hats.
That’s perfectly fine.
What matters is finding someone who understands what your project actually needs — and can deliver it.
The One Line Summary
UX designers make it work.
UI designers make it beautiful.
Web designers make it real.
Now you know the difference.
And next time you’re hiring — you’ll know exactly who to look for.
Start your search on Fiverr and use the skill filters to find the right match for your specific project.
Because now you know the difference between what you’re looking for.
